10.1016/j.jmp.2005.02.001
The quantitative relationship between the latency of response and the luminance of a visual signal is of great interest in the reaction time (RT) literature ( Pins & Bonnet, 1996; Schweickert, Dahn, & McGuigan, 1988; Teichner & Krebs, 1972; Vicars, 1969 ). Among the most widely cited functional forms is the so-called  Piéron's law  ( Luce, 1986; Piéron, 1952 ). 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         Other functional forms have been suggested too. For instance, in  Bartlett and MacLeod's (1954)  study, the following hyperbolic function was proposed: 
                               
                                  
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     i 
                                     ) 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           B 
                                           log 
                                           
                                              
                                                 i 
                                              
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       i 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       * 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     + 
                                     R 
                                     , 
                                  
                               
                            in which  B  is a slope constant,  R  is an irreducible minimum, and  
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                                  
                                     * 
                                  
                               
                             is “a limiting flash intensity at which RT approaches infinity” (1954, p. 309). It has been shown that Bartlett and MacLeod's RT data can be fit by Piéron's law as well (see  Vaughan, Costa, & Gilden, 1966 ). 
                       Piéron wrote: “the latencies decrease from the threshold and tend towards an irreducible limit …The reducible part of the times is inversely proportional to the stimulus intensity raised to a certain power …” (1952, p. 352).

Note that since the RT is always regarded as a random variable, the term “latencies” in this context is practically interchangeable with either the median RT (e.g.  Ueno, 1976; Vaughan et al., 1966 ) or the mean RT (e.g.,  Mansfield, 1973; Piéron, 1952 ). Let  i , measured on a ratio scale, denote the signal intensity. In mathematical terms, we express Piéron's law as a function of  i : 
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               Mean or Median RT 
                               ( 
                               i 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               R 
                               + 
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                                  
                                     - 
                                     β 
                                  
                               
                               C 
                               , 
                            
                         
                      in which  R ,  
                         β 
                       and  C  are positive parameters, with  R  the “irreducible limit” (in Piéron's terms), 
                         2 
                      
                      
                         2 
                         In the RT literature, various names have been attached to  R , such as the “irreducible minimum,” the “mean residue,” or the “nonsensory factor” (see  Luce, 1986 , p. 59). 
                       
                      
                         β 
                       the exponent parameter, and  C  a dimensional constant. The application of Piéron's law to signals from the mesopic or photopic range of luminance in the visual domain shows estimates of the exponent parameter  
                         β 
                       consistently between 0.30 and 0.35 ( Mansfield, 1973; Piéron, 1952; Vaughan et al., 1966 ). 
                         3 
                      
                      
                         3 
                         Note that in Piéron's original formulation, the stimulus intensity is presented using a ‘threshold unit’  
                               i 
                               / 
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                                  
                                     * 
                                  
                               
                            , in which  
                               
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                                  
                                     * 
                                  
                               
                             is the threshold intensity. As can be easily seen from Eq.  (1) , this does not have any impact on  
                               β 
                            .

In the literature, there are some studies on modeling the underlying RT process to obtain a Piéron's law-type of formulation. For instance,  Logan (1992)  proposed a theory of “instances of practice” in his study of automaticity to motivate the use of the Weibull as a suitable distribution for RTs. Together with a ‘horse race’-type assumption on the ‘instances,’ he predicted a power function decrease in RTs as the amount of practice increases.  Hsu (2004)  also motivated a similar but more general latency mechanism in the simple RT situation. Recently,  Stafford and Gurney (2005)  examined a variety of response mechanisms and showed that they all lead to the same pattern of reaction time that has a Piéron's law type of formulation.

We have two comments on Piéron's law. (i) Piéron's law only deals with the effect of the signal intensity on the mean or the median of the RT distribution. It offers no information regarding the effect of the signal intensity on other aspects of the distribution (e.g., other RT percentiles). Evidence has shown that for a given background intensity, both the mean and the variance of the RT distribution decrease as the signal intensity increases (see  Luce, 1986 ). Thus, it is conceivable that the same form of Piéron's law holds not only for the central tendency of the RT distribution, but also for other percentiles in the RT distribution.

(ii) The detectability of the signal is jointly influenced by the signal intensity and the background intensity. However, the background intensity is not specified explicitly in Piéron's law. In fact, in studies of this kind, the background surrounding the target signal was always kept at a very dim level, such as  
                         0.02 
                         
                         cd 
                         / 
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      . There is evidence that the inverse relation between the mean or the median RT and the signal intensity also holds in different background settings ( Bartlett & MacLeod, 1954; Roufs, 1974; Vicars & Lit, 1975 ). One might ask whether the same form of Piéron's law still holds if the background is set at a different intensity level. If it holds, one might also ask whether the parameters, especially  
                         β 
                      , are invariant across those different background intensity conditions. In other words, how do we include the role of the background intensity in the original law?

On the basis of the above comments, we generalize Piéron's law. The aim of this work is to determine theoretically the dependence of the parameters  
                         β 
                       and  C  on the background intensity in the generalized Piéron's law under two additional assumptions, one closely related to the near-miss to Weber's law (see  Doble, Falmagne, & Berg, 2003; Falmagne, 1994 ) and the other closely related to the generalized Fechner–Thurstone-type representability considered in  Falmagne (1985, 1994) . In particular, we show that under a near-miss to Weber's law-type assumption for RTs and a Fechner–Thurstone-type representation, the functional form of  
                         β 
                       in the generalized Piéron's law can be determined. Most of the theoretical statements presented in this paper are results of applications of the functional equation techniques studied in  Aczel and Falmagne (1999) .

Let us first describe the close relationship between the RT random variable and the RT percentiles.

                            Remarks 1 
                            (1) We do not assume that all pairs of stimuli could be used in an idealized experiment. In particular,  i  in the threshold region of  b  is not practical since it would result in many error responses due to stimulus uncertainty.  Pins and Bonnet (2000)  argued that when using Piéron's law to fit the RT data near threshold, the exponent appears to be much higher than the one fitted for suprathreshold intensities.

Regarding our comment (i), we assume that Piéron's law can be generalized to all RT percentiles. This assumption is (at least) supported by the fact that Piéron's law is fairly successful in fitting the median RT, an index widely used by psychologists. Regarding (ii), we specify in Piéron's law the interdependence between the parameters and the background intensity. The generalization is as follows:

                            Remark 2 
                            We assume that the ‘irreducible limit’  R  does not depend on the background intensity. This assumption is supported fairly well in the literature (see  Ejima & Ohtani, 1989; Roufs, 1974; Vicars & Lit, 1975 ). 
                                  7 
                               
                               
                                  7 
                                  One exception is  Bartlett and MacLeod's (1954)  study, where the estimated irreducible limit increases as the background intensity increases. 
                               
                            
                            In the literature, the RT random variable  T  is commonly decomposed additively into a signal-dependent component  D  and a signal-independent component  R , i.e.,  
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  =

                                  +

                                ( Dzhafarov, 1992; Luce, 1986 ). In the following, we describe a similar formulation of the generalized Piéron's law consistent with the notions of random variables. 
                            We can think of the irreducible limit as the signal-independent random variable  R . Thus,  
                                  R 
                                  ( 
                                  π 
                                  ) 
                                becomes the percentile

                                        π

                                  β

                                  π

We assume that the ‘irreducible limit’  R  does not depend on the background intensity. This assumption is supported fairly well in the literature (see  Ejima & Ohtani, 1989; Roufs, 1974; Vicars & Lit, 1975 ). 
                                  7 
                               
                               
                                  7 
                                  One exception is  Bartlett and MacLeod's (1954)  study, where the estimated irreducible limit increases as the background intensity increases.

In the literature, the RT random variable  T  is commonly decomposed additively into a signal-dependent component  D  and a signal-independent component  R , i.e.,  
                                  
                                     T 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                                ( Dzhafarov, 1992; Luce, 1986 ). In the following, we describe a similar formulation of the generalized Piéron's law consistent with the notions of random variables.

We can think of the irreducible limit as the signal-independent random variable  R . Thus,  
                                  R 
                                  ( 
                                  π 
                                  ) 
                                becomes the percentile  
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                  
                               . An appealing model incorporating the two random variables  R  and  D  is to assume that they are perfectly stochastically interdependent, especially, that they are both strictly increasing functions of a single random variable. With  
                                  β 
                                  ( 
                                  π 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                                defined above, we assume that the source of the randomness of  D  comes from  C , thus the function  
                                  C 
                                  ( 
                                  π 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                                becomes the percentile  
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                  
                                  ( 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                               . Moreover, since the variability of  R  is conceptually less than that of any of the observed RT random variables, it is reasonable to assume that the second term on the right-hand side of Eq.  (2)  is strictly increasing in  
                                  π 
                               . 
                                  8 
                               
                               
                                  8 
                                  This means that more constraints are imposed on  C . For example, if assuming differentiability, it would mean that the derivative of  C  with respect to  
                                        π 
                                      is ‘sufficiently large’:

The new generalization then reads

                                              C 
                                           
                                           
                                              π

                                        b 
                                        ) 
                                        , 
                                     
                                  
                               a formulation which is closely related to another special case of the additive decomposition of RT:  Dzhafarov's (1992)  single-variate RT decomposition model, where a perfect positive stochastic interdependence between  R  and  D  is also assumed ( Dzhafarov, 1992; Dzhafarov & Rouder, 1996 ). In the following, we discuss this decomposition model in more detail.

Dzhafarov (1992) , in his theoretical study of the structure of simple RT to signals forming a “unidimensional strength continuum,” discussed a general framework of additive decompositions of RT into a signal-dependent component and a signal-independent component (see  Dzhafarov & Rouder, 1996 ). This decomposition, when applied to our situation, is  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           T 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     R

                                           D 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         in which  
                            
                               R 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                          are random variables representing, respectively, the signal-independent component and the signal-dependent component resulting from the joint influence of  i  and  b . Under some assumptions regarding the asymptotic differentiability of the signal-dependent component, Dzhafarov discussed what he called the “simplest” model of the additive decomposition: the “single-variate RT model,” where the two components are perfectly stochastically interdependent (see the “Asymptotic Differentiability Assumption” on p. 240 and Eqs. (7a)–(7c) on p. 243 of  Dzhafarov, 1992 ). In the current context, it means that  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        T

                            π

                                  π

                            b

At first glance this formulation has a close resemblance to Eq.  (3) . Nonetheless, we have proposed in the generalized Piéron's law that  
                            β 
                          is also a function of  
                            π 
                         . This assumption is fundamentally different from one of the basic assumptions in Dzhafarov's additive decomposition where the postulate of asymptotically proportional rates of any two percentiles indicates that  
                            s 
                            ( 
                            i 
                            , 
                            b 
                            ) 
                          is a deterministic function of  i  but not of  
                            π 
                         . It remains an open problem to postulate reasonable mathematical assumptions from which the generalized Piéron's law can be derived. Note that whether the function  
                            β 
                          varies with  
                            π 
                          is an empirical question. In the RT literature, very few studies have shed light on this topic. While  Dzhafarov's (1992)  experimental data 
                            9 
                         
                         
                            9 
                            Note that the data were obtained from a different experimental situation where the participants respond to instantaneous changes in spatial position of a small light source. Thus, the “unidimensional strength continuum” considered in the experiment is the amplitude of the displacement, which is very different in nature from the intensity of the signal. 
                          suggest that  
                            s 
                            ( 
                            i 
                            ) 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  - 
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          for all  
                            π 
                          (i.e.,  
                            β 
                            = 
                            2 
                          does not vary with  
                            π 
                         ), our preliminary experimental results, not reported here, suggest that  
                            β 
                          is a strictly increasing function of  
                            π 
                          (for details, see  Hsu, 2005 ).

Also note that whether to adopt the original version (Eq.  (2) ) or the special formulation (Eq.  (3) ) of the generalized Piéron's law will not change any of the main results presented in this paper. Since the focus of this paper is to study the dependencies of the parameters  
                            β 
                          and  C  on the background intensity, we shall not impose any random variable-type notions on the parameters in the generalized Piéron's law.

The new formulation of Piéron's law is very general. For instance, fixing  b  and setting  
                         π 
                         = 
                         0.5 
                      , Eq.  (2)  reduces to one form of the original Piéron's law. Studying the dependencies of the parameters  
                         β 
                       and  C  on the background intensity from a theoretical viewpoint is the aim of this work. Especially, we are interested in studying the possible functional form of  
                         β 
                      . However, without further assumptions, Eq.  (2)  alone is not sufficient to achieve our goal. In the following, we propose two homogeneity law-type assumptions to narrow down the class of feasible functions for  
                         β 
                       and  C .

The joint impact of the signal intensity and the background intensity on the RT can be investigated from the viewpoint of a homogeneity law. An example of such a homogeneity law is the Weber's law on the “just noticeable difference” in the study of discriminability. Specifically, let  a  be the stimulus intensity,  
                            π 
                          be the probability representing the value of the criterion, and  
                            
                               
                                  Δ 
                               
                               
                                  π 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                          be the smallest increment such that  
                            a 
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  Δ 
                               
                               
                                  π 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                          “just noticeably” exceeds  a  based on the criterion  
                            π 
                         . Weber's law states that for any real number  
                            λ 
                            > 
                            0 
                         ,  
                            
                               
                                  Δ 
                               
                               
                                  π 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            λ 
                            a 
                            ) 
                            = 
                            λ 
                            
                               
                                  Δ 
                               
                               
                                  π 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         —an equation which is homogeneous of degree 1.

In the RT situation, we can ask a similar question: will there be any changes in the participant's RT distribution if we multiply both the signal intensity and the background intensity by the same constant?

In fact, in the study of discriminability, the experimental evidence supporting Weber's law is somewhat unsatisfactory. Accordingly, a more general formulation, which states that  
                            
                               
                                  Δ 
                               
                               
                                  π 
                               
                            
                          is homogeneous of degree  
                            ρ 
                          ( 
                            ρ 
                            > 
                            0 
                         ), has been proposed, to which the name “near-miss to Weber's law” is attached (for more discussion, see  Doble et al., 2003; Falmagne, 1985, 1994 ).

A similar remark also applies to the RT situation: the Weber's law-type assumption is rather restrictive because studies have shown that in the range where the detection threshold for the stimulus increases as the background intensity increases, the corresponding (median) RTs are not the same (e.g.,  Mollon & Krauskopf, 1973 )—the RT shortens as the threshold rises. Accordingly, we propose a near-miss to Weber's law-type assumption for the RT:

The concept of the homogeneity law also can be studied from a different viewpoint, which we now describe.

                            Corollary 1

In  (iii)  of Theorem  1,  suppose also that the generalized Piéron's law  ( cf. Eq.  
                               (2) )  holds. Then

The consequence of  Corollary 1  cannot be neglected; it indicates that the study of the generalized Piéron's law can be used to infer the validity of the near-miss to Weber's law. Given that typical RT data are well described by Piéron's type of formulation, we can focus on the property of the exponent parameter: if  
                            β 
                            ( 
                            π 
                            , 
                            b 
                            ) 
                          is a constant function of the background intensity  b , it will imply that the near-miss to Weber's law holds; if  
                            β 
                            ( 
                            π 
                            , 
                            b 
                            ) 
                          varies with  b , it will rule out the validity of the near-miss to Weber's law.

In the RT literature, several studies, though not in a direct way, have indicated that  
                            β 
                          varies with the background intensity. For instance, from  Roufs's (1974)  RT study on flicker thresholds, one sees that when the eye is dark adapted, the participant's RT decreases with an increase in signal intensity over several log units. However, when the participant is light adapted, nearly the entire decrease of the RTs occurs when the signal intensity is within a small range above the detection threshold; see Figs. 5 and 6 on p. 861 of  Roufs (1974) . Inspection of those figures suggests that the exponent parameter  
                            β 
                          is probably larger in the light background condition than in the dark background condition, if we apply Piéron's law to Roufs's RT data.

Vicars and Lit's (1975)  study (see also  Vicars, 1969 ) on the RTs for the incremental signal intensity change at six background levels provides more convincing evidence: the decrease in RT at the low-contrast range becomes more abrupt as the background illumination level increases (see Fig. 2 on p. 263 of  Vicars & Lit, 1975 ). In their study, the RT value in each intensity condition was represented by the mean value of the median RTs for 4 blocks of 11 RTs each. We have fitted the generalized Piéron's law (cf. Eq.  (2) ) to their RT data using a version of the least-squares method, and the results showed that the estimated values of  
                            β 
                          are (in order from the lowest-contrast to the highest-contrast background conditions) 0.44, 0.74, 0.29, 0.89, 0.75, 6.46 for one participant (with an estimated  R  at 207.5 ms), and 0.41, 0.46, 0.34, 0.80, 1.21, 6.37 for the other participant (with an estimated  R  at 188.2 ms). 
                            11 
                         
                         
                            11 
                            In the RT literature, several methods have been suggested to estimate the parameters of Piéron's law (see  Luce, 1986 ). Unfortunately, as Luce showed in a simulation study (1982, p. 62), none of the methods yield reliable estimates: they all show large variances of the estimated parameters. This topic is currently being investigated and the results will be reported in a follow-up paper (see  Hsu, 2005 ). In the current data fitting procedure, we used an iterative procedure and obtained the least-squares estimates of the parameters simultaneously. 
                          While the above results do not show a clear increasing pattern of  
                            β 
                          as the background intensity increases, the pattern is largely in agreement with our speculation:  
                            β 
                          varies with the background intensity.

To account for this fact, we re-study the assumptions we have. A natural approach is to weaken the homogeneity property of the sensitivity function. The fact that  
                            β 
                            ( 
                            π 
                            , 
                            b 
                            ) 
                          is very likely to vary with  b  suggests that, in view of  Corollary 1 , the near-miss to Weber's law-type assumption (Eqs.  (4)  and  (5) ) is probably too strong. Accordingly, we loosen the homogeneity assumption by stating that the parameter  
                            ρ 
                          in Eq.  (5)  is a function of both  
                            τ 
                          and  
                            π 
                         : 
                            
                               (6) 
                               
                                  ξ 
                                  ( 
                                  τ 
                                  , 
                                  π 
                                  , 
                                  λ 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ρ 
                                        ( 
                                        τ 
                                        , 
                                        π 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  ξ 
                                  ( 
                                  τ 
                                  , 
                                  π 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                                  .

The above assumption is a very reasonable generalization. It states that the signal intensities yielding exactly a  
                            τ 
                          millisecond RT percentile in the different background conditions are related to each other via Eq.  (6) . Furthermore, different values of  
                            τ 
                         's map to different signal intensities, so do  
                            π 
                         's. Thus, the parameter  
                            ρ 
                         , which now is a function of both  
                            τ 
                          and  
                            π 
                         , provides a flexible way to link those signal intensities.

A similar assumption has been proposed by Falmagne and his colleagues in the study of discriminability ( Doble et al., 2003; Falmagne, 1994 ). The relevant experimental work on the intensity discrimination of pure tones was recently reported by  Doble, Falmagne, and Berg (2004) , where they clearly showed that the exponent in the homogeneity equation is not a constant but a function of the discrimination criterion.

However, it turns out that the generalized Piéron's law (cf. Eq.  (2) ) together with the above homogeneity law of the sensitivity function do not force precise functional forms of  
                            β 
                          and  C . Thus, to follow the same line of theoretical analysis, we need more assumptions about RT. In the following, we study the impact of  i  and  b  on the RT from a different theoretical viewpoint.

In the study of discriminability (e.g., a forced-choice paradigm in which a participant's task is to select which one of the two presented stimuli is brighter), a Fechner–Thurstone-type representation (or model) has been proposed for the choice probabilities ( Falmagne, 1985 ). By a ‘Fechner–Thurstone-type representation’ we mean a model generalizing Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment (Case V) ( Falmagne, 1985, 1994 ). This type of representation postulates that the sensory mechanism produces two internal representations of the physical stimuli that can be expressed as quantities, and that the choice probability is determined by the magnitude of the difference of the two internal representations. While such a representation says very little about the processing details of the (latent) mechanism, it does characterize a large family of models of discrimination (see  Falmagne, 1985 , for more discussion). For a more general treatment of the probability-distance hypothesis under the framework of multidimensional Fechnerian scaling, see also  Dzhafarov (2002) .

Analogous to the above Fechner–Thurstone-type representation, we propose that in the simple RT situation, the sensory mechanism produces the same type of internal representations (as those in the discrimination situation) of the physical stimuli (here, the signal and the background). The RT percentiles are functions of the magnitude of the difference of the two internal representations: the greater the difference, the shorter the RT. In the RT literature, one finds similar assumptions regarding the RT and the distance between the percepts associated with the stimuli. For instance, in  Thomas's (1996)  theoretical study on assessing separability and independence of processing models to the same-difference judgment task, a Fechner–Thurstone-type assumption was also used to derive the RT predictions for a class of processing models: the distance-based models.

Our formulation of the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation in the RT situation is the following:

                            Remark 4 
                            This Fechner–Thurstone-type representation differs from Thurstone's Law of Comparative Judgment in three ways: (i)  
                                  Ψ

We now study the impact of the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation on the generalized Piéron's law and on the weaker form of the homogeneity law. The goal is to narrow down the functional forms of the parameters  
                         β 
                       and  C  in the generalized Piéron's law, given the two assumptions specified in Eqs.  (6)  and  (7) .

At the outset, assuming that the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation holds, we show in the following that the functional forms of some of the parameters in the generalized Piéron's law are restricted.

                            which is empirically testable. That is, in practice Eq.  (12)  will be used to fit  simultaneously  all the RT percentiles obtained from the different background conditions. If the equation is valid, one could use it as a tool to guide parsimonious modelling of the RT data. For example, suppose there are four background conditions, with six signal intensities each. Also, suppose nine different RT percentiles are collected for each RT distribution. Thus, there will be a total of 216 data points from the experiment. Fitting the data with Eq.  (12)  would mean using a total of only 32 parameters:

                            , which is also a dimensional constant and has the same scale as  i  (see Eq.  (9) ). This means that whatever numerical value of

                                     *

If the equation is valid, one could use it as a tool to guide parsimonious modelling of the RT data. For example, suppose there are four background conditions, with six signal intensities each. Also, suppose nine different RT percentiles are collected for each RT distribution. Thus, there will be a total of 216 data points from the experiment. Fitting the data with Eq.  (12)  would mean using a total of only 32 parameters:

It turns out the functional form of  
                               β

Before going back to this point, let us study the relationship between the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation and the weaker form of the homogeneity law. We show in the following that under the above two assumptions, the functional forms of  g  and  v  can be determined.

To refresh the reader's memory, we recall in  Table 1 
                       the functional forms of  v  and  g  derived in  Theorems 3  and  4 . It is clear from the table that (i) of  Theorem 3  is consistent with (i) of  Theorem 4 . In particular,  v  has the same functional form in both cases. Note that if the function  g  in (i) of  Theorem 3  and that in (i) of  Theorem 4  are identical, it can be easily shown that  C  in (i) of  Theorem 3  must be a power function. Interestingly, in this case  
                         ρ 
                       in the homogeneity equation of the sensitivity function must be a constant, as shown in (i) of  Theorem 4 . This fact is consistent with  Corollary 1 .

It is also clear from  Table 1  that (ii) of  Theorem 4  sets some constraints on the possible functional forms of  
                         β 
                       shown in (ii) of  Theorem 3 . In fact, if the functions  v  and  g  in (ii) of  Theorem 3  are identical to the corresponding ones in (ii) of  Theorem 4 , we will have

Suppose there are four background conditions, with six signal intensities each. Also, suppose nine different RT percentiles are collected for each RT distribution. Thus, there will be a total of 216 data points from the experiment. Fitting the data with Eq.  (17)  would mean using a total of only 29 parameters:

                               π 
                               ) 
                            's. Thus, Eq.  (17)  provides an even more parsimonious way than Eq.  (12)  to summarize the RT data.

In this paper, we considered a more general formulation of Piéron's law, in which some of the parameters, including the exponent parameter  
                         β 
                       and the dimensional constant  C , were allowed to vary with the percentile rank  
                         π 
                       and the background intensity  b . Moreover, a Weber's law-type assumption for RTs and a homogeneity law (for the sensitivity function  
                         ξ 
                      ) were considered. In  Theorem 1 , we not only showed the equivalence of the Weber's law and the homogeneity law, but also specified the equivalent forms of the function  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               π 
                            
                         
                      , which in turn set severe constraints on  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         C 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       in the generalized Piéron's law, as shown in  Corollary 1 . In particular, it implied that  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       does not vary with  b .

Experimental results from several researchers indicated that  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       varies with  b . To account for this fact, we weakened the homogeneity law by letting the parameter  
                         ρ 
                       in the homogeneity equation be a function:  
                         ξ 
                         ( 
                         τ 
                         , 
                         π 
                         , 
                         λ 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               ρ 
                               ( 
                               τ 
                               , 
                               π 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                         ξ 
                         ( 
                         τ 
                         , 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         . 
                       However, this assumption together with the generalized Piéron's law were not sufficient to specify the functional forms of  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         C 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      . Accordingly, we introduced one more assumption—a Fechner–Thurstone-type representation. Under this representation, we showed in  Theorem 3  that both  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         C 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       in the generalized Piéron's law are “separable,” providing a systematic and parsimonious way to summarize the experimental data.

We also showed in  Theorem 4  that under the weaker form of the homogeneity law,  v  and  g  in the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation were either log functions or log–log functions. These constraints were helpful in solving for the functional form of  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       in  Theorem 3 . Specifically, we showed in  Corollary 2  that under the framework of the homogeneity law-type assumption and the Fechner–Thurstone-type representation, if the generalized Piéron's law holds, with  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       a nonconstant function of  b , then  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       must have the following form: for any  
                         π 
                      ,  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                         = 
                         M 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                         / 
                         log 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               * 
                            
                         
                         / 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      .

We now turn to a brief discussion of the statistical issue. While the least-squares method can be used to estimate the parameters of the generalized Piéron's law, no corresponding statistical test is available to give a goodness of fit. For instance, for a given  b , the estimated values of  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       ( 
                         π 
                         ∈ 
                         ] 
                         0 
                         , 
                         1 
                         [ 
                      ) using the least-squares method might be close to each other, which may be interpreted as  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                       being independent of  
                         π 
                      . Furthermore, the generalized Piéron's law specifies that Eq.  (2)  holds for all  
                         π 
                         ∈ 
                         ] 
                         0 
                         , 
                         1 
                         [ 
                      . Thus, there is an empirical issue about how many least-squares procedures should be performed when the fit of Eq.  (2)  is tested.

It is now argued that in principle, a likelihood ratio test of Eq.  (2)  is applicable.

For each  b , suppose that there are a total of  k  signal conditions. Setting

                               I

                      i.e., for each  b , the RT percentiles in other signal conditions are all determined by the corresponding RT percentiles in the  
                         i 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       condition and by the parameters  
                         R 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         ) 
                       and  
                         β 
                         ( 
                         π 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ) 
                      . Similar but more complicated forms consistent with  Theorem 3  (and  Corollary 2 ) can be obtained based on Eq.  (12)  (and Eq.  (17) ). These models are testable in the sense of nested likelihood ratios.

However, the interdependence among the multinomial distributions via Eq.  (18)  (and other similar forms based on Eqs.  (12)  and  (17) ) complicates the likelihood ratios—the asymptotic distributions of the likelihood functions may not be Chi-square distributed with appropriate degrees of freedom. Simulation studies will be helpful in understanding the applicability of the multinomial goodness-of-fit test.

Recently,  Brown and Heathcote (2002)  introduced a method for assessing the adequacy of parametric regression models based on nonparametric regression and the bootstrap. This method, when applied to the quantile maximum-likelihood estimates mentioned above, may help to solve the statistical issue. This topic is currently been investigated (see  Hsu, 2005 ).

The following lemma and proposition will be used in most of the proofs presented later in the appendix.

Replacing  g  in Eq.  (11)  by its functional form in Eq.  (15)  and knowing that  
                               δ 
                               = 
                               - 
                               φ 
                             (see  Table 1 ), we obtain, with  
                               M 
                               ( 
                               π 
                               ) 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ℓ 
                                     ( 
                                     π 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                     θ 
                                     ε 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     β 
                                     ( 
                                     π 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           M 
                                           ( 
                                           π 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        
                                           log 
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 b 
                                              
                                              
                                                 * 
                                              
                                           
                                           / 
                                           b 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            The monotonicity of  
                               β 
                             is determined by the sign of  M  and the bound of  
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                                  
                                     * 
                                  
                               
                            :  
                               β 
                             is strictly increasing in  b  if  
                               M 
                               ( 
                               π 
                               ) 
                               > 
                               0 
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                  
                                  
                                     * 
                                  
                               
                               > 
                               b 
                             for all values of  b , and is strictly decreasing otherwise. □
